turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: I have the opinion of the Court in Case 23-5572, Fischer versus United States. This case is about the indictment of Petitioner Joseph Fischer for his actions on January 6, 2021. On that day while Congress met to certify the votes of the 2020 presidential election, a crowd of supporters of then President Donald Trump gathered outside the Capitol. Some people forced their way into the building, breaking windows and assaulting police officers. The certification of the vote was delayed as a result. Fischer is alleged to have been among those who invaded the Capitol. Fischer was charged in an indictment with various crimes. The only one that concerns us today is a charge that he violated 18 U.S.C. Section 1512(c)(2). Now, Section 1512(c) has two subsections. Subsection (c)(1) imposes criminal liability on anyone who corruptly "alters, destroys, mutilates, or conceals a record, document, or other object, or attempts to do so with the intent to impair the object’s integrity or availability for use in an official proceeding." Subsection (c)(2) extends that prohibition to anyone who otherwise obstructs, influences, or impedes any official proceeding or attempts to do so. Now, the question is whether (c)(2) should be read as a freestanding provision, making it a crime to obstruct an official proceeding by any means, so long as the conduct is not already covered by (c)(1). Under this reading, someone could be prosecuted under (c)(2) for obstructing a proceeding by burning down the building where it was being held or blockading the roads leading to the building. Or should (c)(2) be read in light of its connection to (c)(1). (c)(1) prohibits particular ways of impairing records, documents, or other objects used in a proceeding. (c)(2) may be read as covering additional ways of interfering with the evidence in a proceeding, for example, by forging evidence. (c)(2) could also extend the prohibited interference with evidence beyond records, documents, or objects to things such as witness testimony. Maybe Congress wanted to criminalize all conduct that obstructs an official proceeding in Section 1512(c), gave a few examples in (c)(1), and left the rest to be swept up by (c)(2). This all-encompassing interpretation of (c)(2) is what the government asks us to adopt. But (c)(2) could have a narrower reach if Congress designed it to fill any gaps that might exist in (c)(1). Today we reject the government’s sweeping interpretation of (c)(2). We hold that (c)(2) does not cover every other way that a person might obstruct an official proceeding. It instead closes whatever gaps there might be in (c)(1). Our conclusion follows two principles of statutory interpretation. First, the canon of noscitur a sociis says that a word is given more precise content by its neighbors, and the canon of ejusdem generis teaches that a general term is often defined by looking at the specific examples that come before it. Consider an everyday example. A zoo might post a sign that reads do not pet, feed, or throw objects at the animals, or otherwise disturb them. Now, if a visitor eats lunch in front of a gorilla or talks to a friend near its enclosure, has he obeyed the rule? Of course. The specific examples of impermissible conduct are all about direct harassment of the animals. Merely eating or talking is so unlike the petting, feeding, or throwing objects at an animal that it would be implausible to assume that they were prohibited. The same basic idea applies to Section 1512(c). Subsection (c)(1) includes many specific examples of prohibited actions that have the intent to impair an object’s integrity or availability for use in an official proceeding. The most sensible way to read the statute is that those examples help define the scope of (c)(2). And if we ignored those examples and read (c)(2) as broadly as the government asks, (c)(2) would swallow (c)(1), leaving nothing for that narrow provision to do. It makes sense to read (c)(2) as limited by (c)(1) in light of the statutory history. Congress enacted this statute in the Sarbanes-Oxley Act to address a particular problem. In an earlier form the statute made it a crime to persuade another person to shred documents, but curiously failed to punish a person for destroying records himself. The Enron scandal revealed that loophole. Even though Enron’s auditor had destroyed documents, prosecutors had to prove that higher-ups convinced someone else to shred them. So Section 1512(c) was passed to plug this gap. It would be odd to conclude that in doing so Congress actually created a catch-all that reaches far beyond the document shredding scenarios that prompted the legislation. Reading subsection (c)(2) as a freestanding provision, not limited by (c)(1) would also make many other anti-obstruction provisions in the Federal Code unnecessary. And it would erase the fine distinctions Congress drew among different types of obstructive conduct in Section 1512. That section prohibits nearly two dozen means of committing obstruction, with penalties ranging from three years to life in prison. Now, if (c)(2) covered essentially all obstruction, many offenses that Congress assigned proportionate penalties to would all fall under the 20-year maximum in Section (c)(2). Even stranger, an unlimited reading of (c)(2) would impose decades in prison for conduct like protesting and lobbying. Now, our usual practice in obstruction cases has been to avoid reading provisions like (c)(2) to create a cover all statute. There is no reason to depart from that approach today. The judgment of the Court of Appeals is vacated and the case is remanded for further proceedings consistent with our opinion. On remand, the Court of Appeals may assess the sufficiency of Fischer’s indictment in light of our interpretation of Section 1512(c)(2). Justice Jackson has filed a concurrence. Justice Barrett has filed a dissent, joined by Justice Sotomayor and Justice Kagan.
